title: mechanical
main_content: we hypothesized that mechanical forces exerted on the cell/nucleus may cause stable changes in gene expression and tumour cell behaviour. To test this hypothesis,

outcome:
we adapted a system to confine human mela-
noma cells (A375 cell line) in vitro at predefined heights (3 µm) using a
polydimethylsiloxane piston and micropatterned coverslips13 (Fig. 1l).
predicted_context: Established in vitro cell confinement systems, such as employing polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) micro-devices to restrict cells to specific vertical heights, are widely validated methods for applying precise mechanical compressive loads to the cell and its nucleus. Previous studies have successfully utilized these spatial constraint models to demonstrate how physical compression induces nuclear deformation, alters chromatin architecture, and drives specific mechanotransduction pathways in a highly reproducible manner.
predicted_outcome: we will culture melanoma cell lines in a controlled in vitro PDMS-based confinement device to apply static compressive force (e.g., restricting cells to a 3 μm height), and subsequently perform RNA-sequencing (RNA-seq) and Assay for Transposase-Accessible Chromatin using sequencing (ATAC-seq) to map the resulting transcriptional and epigenetic adaptations.
